planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Tiffany is a mid-to-late season camellia blooming January through March in zone 9, providing winter interest when many other landscape plants are dormant.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Tiffany is a magnificent large-flowered Camellia japonica cultivar producing extremely large (4.5–5 inch), semi-double to loose peony-form flowers in a distinctive warm orchid-pink to light lavender-pink color with slightly wavy, cupped petals surrounding a prominent cluster of bright-yellow stamens. The large, open, somewhat informal flower form and the distinctive orchid-pink color (neither the deep red of Mathotiana nor the pure pink of Debutante, but a warm intermediate with lavender tones) make Tiffany instantly recognizable and uniquely beautiful among red and pink camellia cultivars. Tiffany was selected from a seedling in California and has become one of the most popular and widely grown large-flowered japonica cultivars in American gardens. The blooms are produced mid to late season (January through March in zone 9) on an upright, vigorous shrub that can reach 6–12 feet with excellent annual growth rate. As with all Camellia japonica cultivars in zone 9, plant in dappled shade or morning sun with afternoon shade; consistent moisture and acidic soil are essential for healthy growth and maximum bloom production. Supplement iron with chelated iron fertilizer if leaves show yellowing between veins (chlorosis) — a common sign of iron deficiency in alkaline Gulf South soils.

common_name: Moon Bay Nandina (Compact Mounding Heavenly Bamboo)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter through spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Moon Bay is a compact, naturally mounding form that requires little to no pruning — it stays under 3 feet without regular shearing.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Moon Bay is a compact, naturally mounding nandina selection staying 18–30 inches tall — smaller and more tightly formed than Gulf Stream or Harbour Dwarf, making it the most compact of the commonly available dwarf nandina cultivars. The graceful, fine-textured, finely divided foliage is a beautiful glossy-green through summer, turning vivid orange-red to scarlet-red in fall and winter when cold temperatures arrive. Moon Bay's compact, mounding form and outstanding fall/winter color combination make it an exceptionally practical and ornamental foundation shrub for zone 9 gardens where its reliability, low maintenance, and multi-season interest make it one of the most useful small evergreen shrubs in the landscape palette. Like all nandinas, Moon Bay is remarkably adaptable — tolerating clay, drought once established, heat, humidity, and partial shade without significant loss of performance or appearance. The foliage compound texture, graceful habit, and outstanding winter red color make it visually effective in all four seasons. Note: Nandina domestica berries are toxic to birds and the plant has invasive potential in some Gulf South natural areas — plant in contained garden settings away from natural areas.

common_name: Bridal Wreath Spiraea (Plena Old Fashioned Bridal Wreath)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter or very early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Spiraea prunifolia blooms on old wood — prune only immediately after flowering to preserve next year's flower buds.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Spiraea prunifolia, the Bridal Wreath Spiraea, is one of the oldest ornamental shrubs in continuous cultivation in American gardens — grown since the early nineteenth century for its exceptional late winter to early spring flower display of small, perfectly double, white, button-like flowers arranged in dense rows along each long, slender, arching branch before any foliage appears. The distinctive flower arrangement on the bare stems is unlike any other spiraea: dozens of small, round, double flowers line up in rows along the branch length from base to tip, covering the entire structure with a lacy white effect. The fully double, non-fading white flowers persist longer than single-flowered types. Unlike the more common Vanhoutte spiraea (S. × vanhouttei), Spiraea prunifolia has glossy, deep-green, relatively small leaves that turn attractive orange-red to burgundy in fall — providing outstanding fall color that S. × vanhouttei lacks. Spiraea prunifolia grows somewhat more upright than Vanhoutte (5–7 feet tall, wider than tall) with a graceful arching form. In zone 9, it blooms in late February through March — one of the earliest spring-blooming shrubs in the landscape. Like Vanhoutte, it must be pruned only immediately after flowering; pruning in fall or winter removes the old-wood flower buds essential for the spring display.

common_name: Emily Bruner Holly (Large Red-Berried Hybrid Holly)

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Emily Bruner requires a male pollinator (James Swan or other Ilex × koehneana male) nearby for heavy berry production.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Emily Bruner is one of the finest large-statured evergreen holly trees for the Gulf South — a hybrid between Ilex latifolia (lusterleaf holly) and Ilex aquifolium (English holly) that combines the large, deep-green, dramatically lustrous leaves of the former with the heavy red berry production and classic holly appearance of the latter. The large, glossy, finely spined leaves (3–4 inches long) create an exceptionally handsome texture — more coarse and more dramatic than American holly (Ilex opaca) and much bolder and glossier than Chinese holly (Ilex cornuta). The tree grows into a broad, pyramidal to oval form reaching 10–25 feet tall with heavy, persistent red berry clusters that develop after cross-pollination from a male plant (James Swan is the recommended male pollinator for Emily Bruner). The massive red berry clusters that develop in October through December are among the most spectacular berry displays of any landscape holly — birds consume the berries through winter. Emily Bruner is excellent as a specimen tree, screening plant, or large accent in mixed shrub borders. The outstanding year-round appearance and heavy berry production make it one of the finest alternatives to American holly for large-scale landscape use in zone 9.

planting.note: Transplant onion sets or starts in November through January in zone 9 in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0) soil in full sun; Granex is a short-day onion requiring 12 hours or fewer of daylight to form bulbs — it is the correct onion type for Gulf South gardens and bulbs where days are short during the winter growing season.

directions: Transplant onion sets or starts in November through January in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Granex is the foundational sweet onion cultivar behind the famous Vidalia onion — a short-day, mild, sweet onion variety grown in the Vidalia, Georgia, area since the 1940s that became the basis for the legally protected Vidalia Onion designation. The Granex onion (and closely related Granex 33 and similar cultivars) produces round to slightly flattened, large, sweet, mild bulbs with high sugar content and low pyruvic acid — a combination that creates the characteristic sweetness and lack of pungency that makes Vidalia-type onions so prized for fresh eating. Granex is a short-day onion (bulbs when day length reaches 12 hours or less), making it the correct onion type for Gulf South gardens where the short winter days trigger bulbing in March through May. Long-day onion varieties (grown in the northern US) will not bulb in the Gulf South — short-day varieties (Granex, Texas 1015, Red Creole) are essential for zone 9 production. In zone 9, transplant November through January and harvest when the tops begin to fall over (May–June), indicating full bulb development. The mild, sweet Granex onion is outstanding for fresh salads, burgers, and raw applications where a sweet onion is desired.
